topic: A musician hears their song being performed by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The velvet curtain of the small club felt suffocating tonight. Liam stood near the back, nursing a lukewarm beer, his gaze fixed on the stage where Sarah was pouring her voice into the melody. It was *his* song, the one he’d poured sleepless nights and aching vulnerability into, now being spun by someone else’s hands. A tightness constricted his chest, a dull, heavy ache that settled deep in his bones. He shifted his weight, the worn leather of his jacket creaking under the movement. Every note Sarah sang felt like a tiny, sharp splinter against his skin. He watched her face—a look of pure, unadulterated passion—and a profound sense of inadequacy washed over him. He wanted to reach out, to pull the music back, but his feet remained stubbornly rooted to the dusty floorboards.

[story 2]

I sat huddled in the corner booth, the ambient noise of the bar a dull roar against the silence inside my head. When the band finished, the applause washed over the room, but all I could hear was the echo of my own melody, now filtered through another person’s interpretation. My hands were clenched so tightly around my glass that my knuckles were white. I traced the condensation running down the side of the tumbler, the cold liquid doing little to soothe the heat rising in my throat. It was unfair, watching that fragile expression bloom on the singer’s face, a reflection of the raw feeling I’d tried so desperately to capture but never quite managed to translate into something real enough. I just wanted to dissolve into the shadows, to become invisible until the performance was over.

[story 3]

The spotlight caught the guitarist’s silhouette as he took the lead on my track. I leaned against the brick wall outside the venue, the cool night air doing nothing to cool the frantic pulse hammering beneath my ribs. The song flowed, beautiful and soaring, but it wasn't *mine*. It was a ghost wearing my skin, a stranger inhabiting the architecture of my heartache. A tremor ran through my hands; I instinctively pressed my palms against my thighs, trying to anchor myself against the dizzying sense of displacement. I watched the singer sway, completely lost in the music, and a profound wave of quiet resignation settled over me. It was a heavy cloak, this feeling of watching something precious be shared, not by you, but by someone else entirely.
